title: Plastering over a plasterboard patch
subtitle: Scrim the joint, fill in coats, skim flat
excerpt: How to plaster a new plasterboard patch so it is invisible under paint: bedding scrim tape over the joint, building up the fill in two coats of jointing compound, feathering the edges wide, sanding back, and applying a skim coat of finishing plaster.
sourceNotes: Standard UK drywall finishing practice from the British Gypsum White Book and the City & Guilds Plastering Level 2 syllabus. Three-coat jointing compound application unchanged from USG system documentation.

recipeTools.notes: For carrying finishing plaster to the wall for the skim coat.
For applying and polishing the skim coat.
For the initial flatten pass on the skim coat.

aliases: how to plaster a plasterboard patch

glossaryTerms.definition: A self-adhesive fibreglass mesh tape applied across plasterboard joints and patch edges. Bedded under jointing compound or one-coat filler, it bridges the joint and prevents cracking.

A thin (~2 mm) finish coat of fine plaster applied over plasterboard or browning plaster, leaving a smooth surface ready for painting or papering. Multi-finish and Thistle Board Finish are the UK references.

content.text: A plasterboard patch is only invisible when the joint between old and new board is bridged, filled, and skimmed to the same level as the surrounding wall. The sequence has three stages: bed the
scrim tape
over the joint in the first coat of jointing compound, build the fill level in a second coat, sand back flat, then apply a fine
skim coat
to match the surrounding plaster. Each coat needs to dry fully before the next.
What you'll need
Scrim tape.
Self-adhesive fibreglass mesh, 50 mm wide.
Jointing compound.
Pre-mixed compound in a 5 or 20 kg tub. A 5 kg tub covers a typical small patch with coats to spare.
150 mm taping knife and 100 mm filling knife.
The wider knife beds the tape and builds the fill; the narrower knife works the inside corners.
Finishing plaster.
British Gypsum Multi-Finish or Thistle Board Finish. A 25 kg bag covers roughly 10 to 12 square metres at 2 mm thickness.
Plasterer's hawk and trowel.
For the skim coat. The hawk carries a working charge; the trowel applies and polishes.
Sanding pole with mesh screen, 120-grit.
For flattening the fill coats.
First coat: bedding the tape
Step 1, apply scrim tape.

Run the scrim tape along the full length of every joint between the new patch and the existing plasterboard, and along every exposed cut edge. Press it firmly onto the board surface. The self-adhesive backing holds it in position for the first coat.
Step 2, apply the first coat of jointing compound.

Load the 150 mm taping knife with compound and draw a stroke down the full length of the tape, pressing firmly enough to embed the tape and fill the mesh. The compound should be level with the paper face of the plasterboard; do not build it above. Feather the compound out 150 mm on each side of the tape. Allow to dry fully, usually 12 to 24 hours.
Second coat: filling level
Step 3, apply the second coat.

The first coat will have shrunk slightly as it dried. The second coat builds the fill level and extends the feathered edge to 250 mm on each side of the joint. Use the 150 mm knife with light pressure and long strokes, working parallel to the tape. Allow to dry.
Step 4, sand the fill coats.

Sand the fill area with the pole sander and 120-grit mesh screen. Use long strokes parallel to the joints, then cross strokes to even the surface. The aim is to remove the tool marks and high ridges without cutting into the tape. Wipe down the dust with a damp cloth.
Skim coat
Step 5, prime the board and fill.

The bare plasterboard paper and jointing compound will pull moisture from the finishing plaster at different rates, causing uneven suction. Apply a PVA primer at 5:1 dilution over the patch area and allow it to go tacky before skimming. British Gypsum Thistle Bond-It or equivalent.
Step 6, mix the finishing plaster.
Add plaster to clean water (not water to plaster) and mix to a smooth lump-free consistency similar to thick yoghurt. Load the hawk with a working charge.
Step 7, apply the first skim pass.

Transfer plaster from the hawk to the trowel and apply a 2 mm coat over the patch and the immediately surrounding area, spreading outward to blend the edges with the existing plaster. Work in one direction, keeping the trowel at a low angle. Cover the full patch in one continuous movement.
Step 8, tighten and polish.

As the plaster stiffens, return with the dampened trowel held at a slightly higher angle and make tightening passes to compact the surface. Once the plaster is firm but not hard, dampen the trowel with clean water and make final polishing strokes at a steep angle to close the surface smooth. Allow to dry fully before painting, at least 48 hours.
Common mistakes
The tape shows through the skim coat as a ridge.
The fill coats were not feathered wide enough, or the tape was not fully embedded. Sand back the ridge and apply another fill coat with wider feathering.
The skim coat pulls away from the board before setting.
The PVA primer had dried too hard; it should be tacky, not dry, when the plaster goes on. Re-prime and re-skim.
Trowel marks visible under the paint.
The polishing pass was done too late, or with a dirty trowel. Light sanding with 180-grit after full cure removes most tool marks. Do not sand the skim coat while damp.
